divergence_notes: Members > 1: both hosted bake (forall a in A, 2 <= a) into their PrimitiveSet predicate; draft keeps the problem text's definition verbatim (IsPrimitive A = no member of A divides another) and adds the floor as a separate hypothesis (forall a in A, 2 <= a), following the convention of Er35/Li23 ('a set of integers greater than one'). The floor is load-bearing for fidelity: without it, Lean's junk value 1 / (1 * Real.log 1) = 1 / 0 = 0 would make A = {1} satisfy the bound although the informal summand is undefined there, i.e. the naive statement would be Lean-true for the wrong reason.

Domain: Lean's Nat contains 0 while the problem's N starts at 1; the 2 <= a floor also excludes 0 (for which n * log n = 0 and the summand is again junk). Hosted handle this identically via the same floor inside PrimitiveSet.

Summand: draft inlines tsum over the subtype, sum' a : A, 1 / (a * Real.log a), matching the text's 1/(n log n); hosted factor this through erdosWeight n := 1 / (n * Real.log n) and primitiveWeightSum A := sum' a : A, erdosWeight a. Identical values. tsum agrees with the informal sum because Erdos [Er35] proves convergence for every primitive set (nonnegative terms).
Prime side: draft writes {p : Nat | p.Prime} inline; hosted name it primeSet := {p | p.Prime}. Identical.

Conjunct structure: draft asserts IsPrimitive {p | p.Prime} AND the maximisation inequality, following jayyhk's erdos_164 two-conjunct shape; plby's erdos164 carries an extra middle conjunct primitiveWeightSum primeSet = primeWeightSum, which is a definitional rfl about its own local abbreviation and adds no mathematical content. That extra conjunct is the only hosted-vs-hosted disagreement.
Form: draft wraps as answer(True) <-> (FC house style for solved yes/no questions); hosted are bare theorems.

Predicate naming: draft's local Erdos164.IsPrimitive (a ∈ A, b ∈ A, a | b -> a = b) is the Nat-specialisation of FC 1196's Erdos1196.IsPrimitive (which uses Associated over a CommMonoid); on Nat, Associated a b <-> a = b, so the notions coincide. Not reused directly because 1196's def lives in its own problem namespace.
ams_rationale: AMS 11 (number theory): primitive sets under divisibility and sums over primes; matches FC 1196.
